medical.title: Psychological stress alters gut microbes and ages blood stem cells, mouse study suggests
medical.summary: Psychological stress is increasingly recognized as a risk factor for certain health conditions, including cardiovascular disease and diabetes, especially when paired with an impaired immune response. In a study in Cell Stem Cell, researchers describe a mechanism in mice that explains this association: Psychological stress speeds aging-like changes in the body's blood-forming stem cells in the bone marrow—called hematopoietic stem cells—by altering the intestinal microbiota.

medical.title: CBD reduces seizures in drug-resistant pediatric epilepsy, study finds
medical.summary: A new study has found cannabidiol (medicinal cannabis) to be a promising treatment for several drug-resistant forms of pediatric epilepsy, offering renewed hope to families.

medical.title: Persistent vision and hearing health gap for Indigenous Australians
medical.summary: A nationally representative Australian study published in The Lancet Global Health has found that vision impairment remains almost three times more prevalent among Indigenous Australians ages 50 and older than non-Indigenous Australians, while younger Indigenous adults experience substantially higher rates of moderate or worse hearing impairment.

medical.title: Low-cost treatment for multiple sclerosis shown to be as effective as standard therapy
medical.summary: A Norwegian-Swedish clinical trial published in the New England Journal of Medicine demonstrates that rituximab, a low-cost B-cell-depleting therapy, is as effective as ocrelizumab in newly diagnosed relapsing multiple sclerosis (MS). The study, known as OVERLORD-MS, is the first randomized, double-blind, head-to-head trial comparing the two therapies in early MS. Patients from across Norway and Sweden were followed for 30 months with MRI and clinical assessments.

medical.title: Ultra-small magnetoelectric antenna could unlock new generation of implantable devices
medical.summary: A breakthrough in biomedical engineering could help pave the way for tiny implantable devices capable of diagnosing, monitoring and treating a wide range of health conditions. An international team of researchers led by the University of Glasgow has created a new type of ultra-small antenna that can wirelessly transmit data through tissue to external devices.

medical.title: Cancer also knows how to wait: Study uncovers the hidden step between mutation and tumor biomass appearance
medical.summary: The development of cancer is not a process triggered immediately by the emergence of an oncogenic mutation. There is growing evidence for the existence of an intermediate phase—hitherto poorly defined—in which mutated cells remain in a latent state, "accumulating the potential needed to grow, like a biological time bomb," explains Antonio Gentilella, a professor at the Faculty of Pharmacy and Food Sciences at the University of Barcelona and head of the Cancer Metabolism Research Group at the Bellvitge Biomedical Research Institute (IDIBELL), part of the Bellvitge Campus.

medical.title: Common mucus-clearing treatments don't help ICU patients breathe easier and may cause harm, clinical trial finds
medical.summary: For patients struggling to breathe because of acute respiratory failure, clearing mucus from the airways is a routine part of treatment. Mucoactive agents are widely used for this purpose. But after years of clinical use, one question remains: Do mucoactive agents actually help?

medical.title: New biological clues behind coffee's benefit to liver health
medical.summary: In one of the most comprehensive studies of coffee and liver health to date, Cedars-Sinai Health Sciences University investigators linked higher coffee consumption with a lower risk of cirrhosis, liver cancer and liver-related death. The findings, published in Clinical Gastroenterology and Hepatology, also provide new biological evidence that may help explain those associations.

medical.title: What went right this week: an ‘incredible’ medical milestone, plus more
medical.summary: A vaccine ‘almost eliminated’ a type of cancer, the green economy surged, and India halved its smoking rate, plus more The post What went right this week: an ‘incredible’ medical milestone, plus more appeared first on Positive News .

medical.title: Can volunteering abroad build the next generation of global citizens?
medical.summary: Overseas volunteering offer a practical route into global citizenship – one built through classrooms, communities and cultural exchange The post Can volunteering abroad build the next generation of global citizens? appeared first on Positive News .

medical.title: A daily probiotic may help relieve depression and anxiety
medical.summary: A small clinical trial suggests that probiotics may offer a surprising mental health boost for older adults with depression. Seniors who took a daily probiotic alongside their regular antidepressant treatment experienced slightly greater improvements in depression and anxiety symptoms than those who received a placebo.

medical.title: New procedure delivers lasting knee arthritis pain relief without surgery
medical.summary: A minimally invasive treatment that blocks inflammation-driving blood vessels in the knee provided significant pain relief and improved function for osteoarthritis patients, with benefits lasting at least a year. The procedure was safe, highly successful, and could offer a new alternative for people seeking relief before considering knee replacement.

science.title: Honeybee queens push pesticides to eggs to protect themselves over their offspring, research reveals
science.summary: Worker bees are the first line of defense when it comes to removing contamination in honeybee colonies, but a queen has her ways, too. A honeybee queen facing chronic exposure to pesticides will take up that contamination and pass it along to her eggs, a process researchers call maternal offloading.

science.title: Rethinking the governance of human embryo research: Comparing Japan's guidelines with international standards
science.summary: Human embryo models can help researchers study early human development and infertility without relying solely on human embryos. As the technology advances, these models are becoming more complex and can be maintained in culture for longer periods. These developments have raised new questions about how such research should be regulated.

science.title: How heat stress triggers emergency programs in plants
science.summary: Researchers at the Karlsruhe Institute of Technology (KIT) have found how plant cells respond to stress. If their energy supply is disrupted by heat, drought or saline soils, chloroplasts—the cells' powerhouses—send an intracellular distress signal by forming tiny, finger-like projections. This signal specifically activates protection programs that help limit damage. The study, published in Plant Physiology, reveals the function of these previously mysterious structures and presents an approach for making crops more resilient to climate stress.

science.title: Scourge of satellites lighting up the sky could be mitigated with help of ultra-black coating
science.summary: Astrophysicists working to tackle the growing impact of satellite constellations have pioneered a new ultra-black coating as one possible way to mitigate the problem.

science.title: Genomic tool can help measure resilience in Merino sheep
science.summary: A new resilience test for merino sheep is using hereditary markers to help producers identify which animals are better able to cope with stressors in their environment.

science.title: A holoparasitic plant replaces its own genes with host DNA to survive
science.summary: All living organisms are known to inherit genes, DNA sequences that contain instructions for producing specific proteins and performing biological functions, from their parents. In some cases, however, genes can also shift between different species via a process known as horizontal gene transfer (HGT).

science.title: Enriching conversations with toddlers
science.summary: Asking open-ended questions and weaving conversations into everyday activities helps toddlers' communication skills, new research shows. Three recently published University of Otago–Ōtākou Whakaihu Waka studies analyzed the outcomes of the Enhancing Rich Interactions (ENRICH) program, which provides early childhood educators with tools and techniques to help improve toddlers' language, literacy and social skills. Using parent and teacher ratings and video analysis, researchers found the program had a positive impact on toddlers' oral language.

science.title: Quantum semiconductor design could expand search for dark matter
science.summary: Dark matter accounts for 85% of the matter in the universe, but scientists still do not know what it is made of. A study, published in Physical Review Letters, by Rice University researchers proposes a detector design that could help search for axions, hypothetical particles that many physicists think could make up dark matter.

science.title: AI could bring satellite crop monitoring to the world's most vulnerable farms
science.summary: Small farms grow much of the world's food, but from space they are nearly invisible. Their fields are tiny and ill-defined, and the satellite tools built to track crops were designed for the large, uniform fields of industrial agriculture, not the sub-hectare plots that feed many of the world's poorest people.

science.title: Abandoned farmland restored to wildflower meadow without sowing seeds
science.summary: Abandoned farmland can be transformed into wildflower-rich grassland habitat without the need for expensive and labor-intensive seeding, a new study by UCL researchers finds.

science.title: Analog gravity advance offers new insights into Hawking radiation from black holes
science.summary: Hawking radiation is a form of radiation emitted by black holes, as theoretically predicted by Stephen Hawking. It suggests that black holes do not merely swallow matter—as had previously been assumed—but also emit very faint radiation themselves. This radiation has not yet been observed in space; instead, researchers use models in the laboratory that mimic the behavior of black holes.

science.title: Swimming crab trapped in plastic bottle survives two months at sea
science.summary: How did a large crab end up trapped inside a plastic bottle with an opening smaller than its body? Hiroshima University researchers investigated this unusual marine mystery, revealing a lesser-known impact of marine plastic pollution on crustaceans.

environment.title: Bornean ferret badger only lives in Borneo. Could it be a conservation symbol?
environment.summary: The Bornean ferret badger is a small carnivore with the slinky body of a ferret and a face mask like a badger. A new study confirms that it lives only in the mountains of Sabah, a Malaysian state on the island of Borneo. Ferret badgers are nocturnal carnivores, widespread across Southeast Asia, but the Bornean […]

environment.title: Mozambique completes first white rhino breeding population in decades
environment.summary: On June 6, nine female white rhinos arrived in Mozambique’s Zinave National Park following a two-day translocation. Their arrival marks the culmination of nearly 10 years of rhino reintroduction efforts in the park, aimed at rebuilding a viable breeding population of the mammals in Zinave after decades of local extinction. The white rhinos (Ceratotherium simum) […]

environment.title: ‘Flamingo Revolution’ aims to stop Kushner-backed resort on protected Albanian delta
environment.summary: VJOSA-NARTA, Albania — In late April, heavy machinery began moving into the Pishë Poro-Narta protected landscape on Albania’s Adriatic coast without permits or public notice. Bulldozers and excavators felled coastal pine trees, flattened sand dunes, and cut new roads through previously untouched habitat. Then, barbed wire fences went up along the shoreline. The incursion was […]

environment.title: The secret underground system keeping the Grand Canyon alive
environment.summary: Scientists are venturing into the Grand Canyon’s hidden cave networks to solve a mystery: how snowmelt travels underground to supply the park’s vital springs. Their discoveries could help protect the canyon’s water from drought, contamination, and other growing threats.

environment.title: DNA solves 250-year-old mystery of the Seychelles’ lost crocodiles
environment.summary: Scientists have solved the mystery of the Seychelles’ vanished crocodiles using DNA from historic museum specimens. The reptiles were not a unique species after all, but an isolated population of saltwater crocodiles that likely drifted thousands of kilometers across the Indian Ocean.

environment.title: Scottish wrens may be evolving into new species through island gigantism
environment.summary: Tiny birds on remote Scottish islands are undergoing a dramatic evolutionary transformation. Scientists studying four isolated populations of British Wrens discovered that some island birds have grown astonishingly large — with the biggest St Kilda Wrens weighing more than twice as much as the smallest mainland birds. The research suggests these wrens are evolving independently, developing unique songs, appearances, and genetics that may eventually turn them into entirely new species.

environment.title: A New York cemetery was hiding 5.5 million bees underground
environment.summary: A casual walk through an Ithaca cemetery led to the discovery of a gigantic hidden bee population — roughly 5.5 million ground-nesting bees packed beneath the soil. Scientists believe it may be one of the largest bee aggregations ever documented and say the insects are crucial pollinators for apple orchards and other crops. The bees have likely lived there for more than 100 years, thriving in the cemetery’s undisturbed sandy soil.

environment.title: Earth’s orbital wobble triggered rapid climate chaos during the dinosaur age
environment.summary: New research suggests Earth’s climate can swing wildly on surprisingly short timescales — even during hot, ice-free greenhouse periods. By studying ancient sediments from the Late Cretaceous, scientists uncovered repeating climate shifts tied to tiny changes in Earth’s orbital wobble. These cycles may have repeatedly pushed the planet between humid and arid states every few thousand years.

mix_items.title: Honeybee queens push pesticides to eggs to protect themselves over their offspring, research reveals
mix_items.summary: Worker bees are the first line of defense when it comes to removing contamination in honeybee colonies, but a queen has her ways, too. A honeybee queen facing chronic exposure to pesticides will take up that contamination and pass it along to her eggs, a process researchers call maternal offloading.

mix_items.title: Psychological stress alters gut microbes and ages blood stem cells, mouse study suggests
mix_items.summary: Psychological stress is increasingly recognized as a risk factor for certain health conditions, including cardiovascular disease and diabetes, especially when paired with an impaired immune response. In a study in Cell Stem Cell, researchers describe a mechanism in mice that explains this association: Psychological stress speeds aging-like changes in the body's blood-forming stem cells in the bone marrow—called hematopoietic stem cells—by altering the intestinal microbiota.

mix_items.title: Rethinking the governance of human embryo research: Comparing Japan's guidelines with international standards
mix_items.summary: Human embryo models can help researchers study early human development and infertility without relying solely on human embryos. As the technology advances, these models are becoming more complex and can be maintained in culture for longer periods. These developments have raised new questions about how such research should be regulated.

mix_items.title: CBD reduces seizures in drug-resistant pediatric epilepsy, study finds
mix_items.summary: A new study has found cannabidiol (medicinal cannabis) to be a promising treatment for several drug-resistant forms of pediatric epilepsy, offering renewed hope to families.

mix_items.title: Persistent vision and hearing health gap for Indigenous Australians
mix_items.summary: A nationally representative Australian study published in The Lancet Global Health has found that vision impairment remains almost three times more prevalent among Indigenous Australians ages 50 and older than non-Indigenous Australians, while younger Indigenous adults experience substantially higher rates of moderate or worse hearing impairment.

mix_items.title: How heat stress triggers emergency programs in plants
mix_items.summary: Researchers at the Karlsruhe Institute of Technology (KIT) have found how plant cells respond to stress. If their energy supply is disrupted by heat, drought or saline soils, chloroplasts—the cells' powerhouses—send an intracellular distress signal by forming tiny, finger-like projections. This signal specifically activates protection programs that help limit damage. The study, published in Plant Physiology, reveals the function of these previously mysterious structures and presents an approach for making crops more resilient to climate stress.

mix_items.title: Scourge of satellites lighting up the sky could be mitigated with help of ultra-black coating
mix_items.summary: Astrophysicists working to tackle the growing impact of satellite constellations have pioneered a new ultra-black coating as one possible way to mitigate the problem.

mix_items.title: Low-cost treatment for multiple sclerosis shown to be as effective as standard therapy
mix_items.summary: A Norwegian-Swedish clinical trial published in the New England Journal of Medicine demonstrates that rituximab, a low-cost B-cell-depleting therapy, is as effective as ocrelizumab in newly diagnosed relapsing multiple sclerosis (MS). The study, known as OVERLORD-MS, is the first randomized, double-blind, head-to-head trial comparing the two therapies in early MS. Patients from across Norway and Sweden were followed for 30 months with MRI and clinical assessments.

mix_items.title: Genomic tool can help measure resilience in Merino sheep
mix_items.summary: A new resilience test for merino sheep is using hereditary markers to help producers identify which animals are better able to cope with stressors in their environment.

mix_items.title: A holoparasitic plant replaces its own genes with host DNA to survive
mix_items.summary: All living organisms are known to inherit genes, DNA sequences that contain instructions for producing specific proteins and performing biological functions, from their parents. In some cases, however, genes can also shift between different species via a process known as horizontal gene transfer (HGT).

mix_items.title: Ultra-small magnetoelectric antenna could unlock new generation of implantable devices
mix_items.summary: A breakthrough in biomedical engineering could help pave the way for tiny implantable devices capable of diagnosing, monitoring and treating a wide range of health conditions. An international team of researchers led by the University of Glasgow has created a new type of ultra-small antenna that can wirelessly transmit data through tissue to external devices.

mix_items.title: Enriching conversations with toddlers
mix_items.summary: Asking open-ended questions and weaving conversations into everyday activities helps toddlers' communication skills, new research shows. Three recently published University of Otago–Ōtākou Whakaihu Waka studies analyzed the outcomes of the Enhancing Rich Interactions (ENRICH) program, which provides early childhood educators with tools and techniques to help improve toddlers' language, literacy and social skills. Using parent and teacher ratings and video analysis, researchers found the program had a positive impact on toddlers' oral language.

mix_items.title: Quantum semiconductor design could expand search for dark matter
mix_items.summary: Dark matter accounts for 85% of the matter in the universe, but scientists still do not know what it is made of. A study, published in Physical Review Letters, by Rice University researchers proposes a detector design that could help search for axions, hypothetical particles that many physicists think could make up dark matter.

mix_items.title: AI could bring satellite crop monitoring to the world's most vulnerable farms
mix_items.summary: Small farms grow much of the world's food, but from space they are nearly invisible. Their fields are tiny and ill-defined, and the satellite tools built to track crops were designed for the large, uniform fields of industrial agriculture, not the sub-hectare plots that feed many of the world's poorest people.

mix_items.title: Abandoned farmland restored to wildflower meadow without sowing seeds
mix_items.summary: Abandoned farmland can be transformed into wildflower-rich grassland habitat without the need for expensive and labor-intensive seeding, a new study by UCL researchers finds.

mix_items.title: Analog gravity advance offers new insights into Hawking radiation from black holes
mix_items.summary: Hawking radiation is a form of radiation emitted by black holes, as theoretically predicted by Stephen Hawking. It suggests that black holes do not merely swallow matter—as had previously been assumed—but also emit very faint radiation themselves. This radiation has not yet been observed in space; instead, researchers use models in the laboratory that mimic the behavior of black holes.

mix_items.title: Cancer also knows how to wait: Study uncovers the hidden step between mutation and tumor biomass appearance
mix_items.summary: The development of cancer is not a process triggered immediately by the emergence of an oncogenic mutation. There is growing evidence for the existence of an intermediate phase—hitherto poorly defined—in which mutated cells remain in a latent state, "accumulating the potential needed to grow, like a biological time bomb," explains Antonio Gentilella, a professor at the Faculty of Pharmacy and Food Sciences at the University of Barcelona and head of the Cancer Metabolism Research Group at the Bellvitge Biomedical Research Institute (IDIBELL), part of the Bellvitge Campus.

mix_items.title: Common mucus-clearing treatments don't help ICU patients breathe easier and may cause harm, clinical trial finds
mix_items.summary: For patients struggling to breathe because of acute respiratory failure, clearing mucus from the airways is a routine part of treatment. Mucoactive agents are widely used for this purpose. But after years of clinical use, one question remains: Do mucoactive agents actually help?

mix_items.title: New biological clues behind coffee's benefit to liver health
mix_items.summary: In one of the most comprehensive studies of coffee and liver health to date, Cedars-Sinai Health Sciences University investigators linked higher coffee consumption with a lower risk of cirrhosis, liver cancer and liver-related death. The findings, published in Clinical Gastroenterology and Hepatology, also provide new biological evidence that may help explain those associations.

mix_items.title: Swimming crab trapped in plastic bottle survives two months at sea
mix_items.summary: How did a large crab end up trapped inside a plastic bottle with an opening smaller than its body? Hiroshima University researchers investigated this unusual marine mystery, revealing a lesser-known impact of marine plastic pollution on crustaceans.

mix_items.title: Bornean ferret badger only lives in Borneo. Could it be a conservation symbol?
mix_items.summary: The Bornean ferret badger is a small carnivore with the slinky body of a ferret and a face mask like a badger. A new study confirms that it lives only in the mountains of Sabah, a Malaysian state on the island of Borneo. Ferret badgers are nocturnal carnivores, widespread across Southeast Asia, but the Bornean […]

mix_items.title: Mozambique completes first white rhino breeding population in decades
mix_items.summary: On June 6, nine female white rhinos arrived in Mozambique’s Zinave National Park following a two-day translocation. Their arrival marks the culmination of nearly 10 years of rhino reintroduction efforts in the park, aimed at rebuilding a viable breeding population of the mammals in Zinave after decades of local extinction. The white rhinos (Ceratotherium simum) […]

mix_items.title: ‘Flamingo Revolution’ aims to stop Kushner-backed resort on protected Albanian delta
mix_items.summary: VJOSA-NARTA, Albania — In late April, heavy machinery began moving into the Pishë Poro-Narta protected landscape on Albania’s Adriatic coast without permits or public notice. Bulldozers and excavators felled coastal pine trees, flattened sand dunes, and cut new roads through previously untouched habitat. Then, barbed wire fences went up along the shoreline. The incursion was […]

mix_items.title: What went right this week: an ‘incredible’ medical milestone, plus more
mix_items.summary: A vaccine ‘almost eliminated’ a type of cancer, the green economy surged, and India halved its smoking rate, plus more The post What went right this week: an ‘incredible’ medical milestone, plus more appeared first on Positive News .

mix_items.title: Can volunteering abroad build the next generation of global citizens?
mix_items.summary: Overseas volunteering offer a practical route into global citizenship – one built through classrooms, communities and cultural exchange The post Can volunteering abroad build the next generation of global citizens? appeared first on Positive News .

mix_items.title: A daily probiotic may help relieve depression and anxiety
mix_items.summary: A small clinical trial suggests that probiotics may offer a surprising mental health boost for older adults with depression. Seniors who took a daily probiotic alongside their regular antidepressant treatment experienced slightly greater improvements in depression and anxiety symptoms than those who received a placebo.

mix_items.title: New procedure delivers lasting knee arthritis pain relief without surgery
mix_items.summary: A minimally invasive treatment that blocks inflammation-driving blood vessels in the knee provided significant pain relief and improved function for osteoarthritis patients, with benefits lasting at least a year. The procedure was safe, highly successful, and could offer a new alternative for people seeking relief before considering knee replacement.

mix_items.title: The secret underground system keeping the Grand Canyon alive
mix_items.summary: Scientists are venturing into the Grand Canyon’s hidden cave networks to solve a mystery: how snowmelt travels underground to supply the park’s vital springs. Their discoveries could help protect the canyon’s water from drought, contamination, and other growing threats.

mix_items.title: DNA solves 250-year-old mystery of the Seychelles’ lost crocodiles
mix_items.summary: Scientists have solved the mystery of the Seychelles’ vanished crocodiles using DNA from historic museum specimens. The reptiles were not a unique species after all, but an isolated population of saltwater crocodiles that likely drifted thousands of kilometers across the Indian Ocean.

mix_items.title: Scottish wrens may be evolving into new species through island gigantism
mix_items.summary: Tiny birds on remote Scottish islands are undergoing a dramatic evolutionary transformation. Scientists studying four isolated populations of British Wrens discovered that some island birds have grown astonishingly large — with the biggest St Kilda Wrens weighing more than twice as much as the smallest mainland birds. The research suggests these wrens are evolving independently, developing unique songs, appearances, and genetics that may eventually turn them into entirely new species.

mix_items.title: A New York cemetery was hiding 5.5 million bees underground
mix_items.summary: A casual walk through an Ithaca cemetery led to the discovery of a gigantic hidden bee population — roughly 5.5 million ground-nesting bees packed beneath the soil. Scientists believe it may be one of the largest bee aggregations ever documented and say the insects are crucial pollinators for apple orchards and other crops. The bees have likely lived there for more than 100 years, thriving in the cemetery’s undisturbed sandy soil.

mix_items.title: Earth’s orbital wobble triggered rapid climate chaos during the dinosaur age
mix_items.summary: New research suggests Earth’s climate can swing wildly on surprisingly short timescales — even during hot, ice-free greenhouse periods. By studying ancient sediments from the Late Cretaceous, scientists uncovered repeating climate shifts tied to tiny changes in Earth’s orbital wobble. These cycles may have repeatedly pushed the planet between humid and arid states every few thousand years.

joke_ro.text: M-am apucat de ordine in casa. Casa s-a opus.

satire_ro.title: Charlie Ottley va promova Craiova. Cine e cascadorul care îl dublează în scenele cu bătăi între clanuri
